title.emphasis: Part I: The Foundation of Non-Disclaimable Duties in California

section.para: The enforceability of any contract in California is subject to overarching legal principles that prevent agreements from violating public policy or fundamental standards of fairness. These principles form the basis for duties that cannot be simply waived by a clause in a Terms of Service agreement.

title.emphasis: 1.1 The General Duty of Care (California Civil Code § 1714)

section.para: The cornerstone of liability in California is established in Civil Code § 1714(a), which states: "Everyone is responsible, not only for the result of his or her willful acts, but also for an injury occasioned to another by his or her want of ordinary care or skill in the management of his or her property or person".1 This statute codifies a universal duty of care that all persons and entities owe to one another.

This is not an abstract principle; it creates a direct, non-disclaimable obligation for business owners to their patrons.1 A business must exercise reasonable care to keep its premises and operations safe for visitors.11 While a contract may attempt to limit liability, it cannot fully absolve a business of this fundamental duty to act with "ordinary care or skill" to prevent foreseeable harm.12

California law provides a powerful tool for courts to police unfair contracts: the doctrine of unconscionability. A court can refuse to enforce a contract or any of its terms if it finds the agreement to be unconscionable, meaning it is so unfair that it "shocks the conscience".8

listitem.para: Procedural Unconscionability: This focuses on the process of contract formation and the presence of "oppression" or "surprise." It often arises from unequal bargaining power, such as in a "take it or leave it" contract of adhesion where the user has no ability to negotiate terms.9

Substantive Unconscionability: This examines the fairness of the terms themselves, looking for provisions that are "overly harsh" or "so one-sided" as to be unreasonable.9 Examples include severe limitations on the seller's liability or on a consumer's right to seek legal relief.8

section.para: California courts use a "sliding scale" approach: the more procedurally unconscionable the contract formation is, the less evidence of substantive unconscionability is required to invalidate a term, and vice versa.9 This doctrine is particularly relevant for challenging broad liability disclaimers in standard Terms of Service, as these agreements are almost always procedurally unconscionable.
